cards.front: How do you form the tú affirmative command?
cards.back: Use the él/ella present tense form: hablar → habla, comer → come, abrir → abre.
cards.grammarNote: The tú affirmative imperative is the same as the third-person singular present: él habla → ¡habla! The subject pronoun is dropped. Exception: irregular commands include di (decir), haz (hacer), ve (ir), ten (tener).

cards.front: How do you form the usted command?
cards.back: Take the yo present → drop -o → add opposite vowel: habl-o → hable, com-o → coma, abr-o → abra.
cards.grammarNote: The usted command uses the present subjunctive form. -AR verbs take -e endings; -ER/-IR verbs take -a endings. This is the formal register used in medical and professional settings.

cards.front: Open your mouth, please. (formal — doctor to patient)
Rest and drink plenty of water. (formal)
cards.grammarNote: Both 'descanse' and 'tome' are usted commands. Doctors typically use usted with patients for a professional, respectful register.

cards.front: Take this medication twice a day. (formal)
cards.back: Tome este medicamento dos veces al día.

cards.front: How do you form a negative tú command?
cards.back: Use 'no' + the present subjunctive form: no hables, no comas, no abras.
cards.grammarNote: Unlike the affirmative tú command (which uses the él/ella form), the negative tú command uses the subjunctive: ¡habla! but ¡no hables! This is a common mistake.

cards.front: Don't eat anything before the operation. (formal)
cards.back: No coma nada antes de la operación.

cards.grammarNote: Reflexive commands attach the pronoun to the end of the affirmative form: relaja + te = relájate. An accent is usually needed to keep the original stress.
